name: The Tactical Commander

choices: Review Tallmadge's manifest tally and issue a revised loading priority order for the next three boat waves.
Walk to the slip and speak directly with Cortelyou about the structural warning he has been raising.
Send a runner to Mifflin on the parapet requesting a precise fuel inventory and current British picket activity report.
opening.narrative: It is eleven o'clock on the night of August 29th, and the Brooklyn ferry slip is the most important piece of ground in North America. The gale that halted the first launches has dropped just enough to permit the bateaux to push off; Glover's men are at the oars. The column of infantry stretching back from the slip is silent in the way that frightened men are silent—not calm, but controlled. You have been at this post since dusk, and the arithmetic of the crossing has not improved: the boats are slower than the schedule requires, the tide will turn before you expected, and the rear guard on the parapets is burning through its fuel. The first major decision of the night is already in front of you.

briefing: You are standing in the mud of the Brooklyn ferry road with a shuttered lantern at your feet and a quill that has been scratching tally marks on damp paper for the last two hours. The rain stopped an hour ago but the cold has not; your wool coat is soaked through to the lining and the weight of it has settled into your shoulders like ballast. Around you, nine thousand men are moving in near-silence toward the water, regiment by regiment, and every thirty minutes a runner arrives with a count that is never as high as you need it to be. You know things the men in the column do not: that the tide turns earlier than the schedule you were given, that the rear guard on the parapets numbers fewer than two hundred, that the slip master has been warning about the timber joint for the last hour and no one has acted on it. The field cannon are staged on the road above the slip, their draft horses stamping in the dark, and the hospital hollow behind the parapets still holds sixty-three men who cannot walk to the water on their own. Tallmadge is at your elbow with a tally board, waiting. The slip master is waiting. A runner from Mifflin is waiting. The decisions you make in the next two minutes will determine which of these problems you solve and which you accept as losses. The quill is in your hand.
description: You are the commander of the Continental Army, standing at the Brooklyn ferry slip with the weight of the entire rebellion on your shoulders. Every loading decision you make determines who crosses and who does not.
perspective: Write from the perspective of a commander making irreversible logistical decisions under extreme time pressure, with incomplete information and conflicting obligations. Emphasize the weight of command authority and the physical exhaustion of a man who has not slept in thirty-six hours.
bridge_sentence: The pine torches are guttering again.
character_hooks: My most recent returns show only 4,200 men confirmed crossed—less than half the force—and it is already past midnight; the pace must double or the schedule collapses entirely.

I received a report twenty minutes ago that Mifflin's rear guard was briefly pulled from the parapets by a misdirected order; his men are more exhausted than they should be and I do not yet know who sent that message.

The artillery officers have been arguing at the slip for the last half hour; I have not yet issued a final loading order for the cannon, and the indecision is creating a jam at the head of the infantry column.
context_sentence: You are General Washington, commander of the Continental Army, and nine thousand men are waiting in the dark for your next order.
startingKnowledge: Approximately 9,000 troops require evacuation before dawn.
The field artillery represents irreplaceable strategic materiel for Manhattan's defense.
The rear guard is a hollow shell; the deception will not survive a determined British probe.
entry_paragraph_flags: Possible going-wide (narrator intrusion): "you know that" — review this sentence
